food_subgroup: Battered and deep-fried seafood with rice (bento)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tempura class (FOODON_03302051) but no specific entry for salmon-and-prawn tempura bento. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as closest parent for prawn tempura component; no composite bento code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite bento meal code. A0CKP (cooked crustaceans) is used as the closest defensible anchor for the prawn tempura component. Salmon tempura component maps loosely to processed fish category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for salmon and prawn tempura bento as a composite dish.

main_ingredients: tempura batter (wheat flour, egg, cold water)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bento box serving of approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice, 2–3 pieces of salmon tempura (~100 g), 2–3 pieces of prawn tempura (~80 g), and accompanying condiments or pickles (~70 g).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Composite estimation: Tempura, shrimp (SR Legacy 35196) + Salmon, cooked (SR Legacy 15236) + White rice, cooked (SR Legacy 20045); weighted blend for bento proportions
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for a salmon-and-prawn tempura bento. Values are estimated by weighted blending of three USDA SR Legacy entries: shrimp tempura (FDC 35196, ~40% weight contribution for fried seafood components), cooked Atlantic salmon (FDC 15236, ~22% weight), and cooked white rice (FDC 20045, ~38% weight). Proportions reflect a typical 450 g bento: 200 g rice, 100 g salmon tempura, 80 g prawn tempura, 70 g condiments/pickles. Condiment sodium contribution estimated from standard soy-sauce dipping sauce at ~5 ml per serving. All per-100 g values are derived from this weighted composite.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White rice, boiled (GI 64–72); tempura batter contribution estimated from refined wheat flour GI ~70; composite bento GI estimated at 65 (medium) weighted by carbohydrate contribution of rice component
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a salmon-and-prawn tempura bento. The GI of 65 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source: Japanese short-grain white rice, which has a published GI of approximately 64–72 (mean ~68) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. The tempura batter (refined wheat flour) contributes a minor carbohydrate fraction with GI ~70. Fat and protein from salmon and prawn tempura slow gastric emptying, moderating the overall glycemic response; a conservative composite GI of 65 (medium category) is applied. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 101.25) / 100 ≈ 65.8, rounded to 66.1 accounting for condiment carbohydrates.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy-sauce dipping sauce and seasoned rice
hypertension.guidance: Each bento serving provides an estimated 1,395 mg of sodium (~61% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association), primarily from soy-sauce-based condiments; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce or limit dipping sauce use to control sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from white rice and refined tempura batter
type2_diabetes.guidance: The bento's estimated glycemic load of ~66 per serving is high for a single meal; people with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion to half, substituting brown rice where available, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from salmon offset by deep-frying oil and sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salmon provides cardioprotective EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids, but deep-frying in vegetable oil adds approximately 6.2 g of total fat per 100 g and the soy-sauce condiment contributes significant sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption to balance the omega-3 benefit against the sodium and fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salmon and Prawn Tempura Bento (三文鱼炸虾天妇罗便当; Japanese: サーモンと海老の天ぷら弁当) is a Japanese-style bento box meal consisting of crispy deep-fried salmon fillets and whole prawns coated in a light, airy tempura batter, served alongside a generous portion of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. Tempura batter is made from cold water, egg, and low-gluten wheat flour, producing a delicate, golden crust that contrasts with the moist, flaky salmon and the firm, sweet prawn flesh inside. The dish is typically accompanied by a small dish of tentsuyu dipping sauce (dashi, mirin, and soy sauce) and may include pickled vegetables or a simple salad. In Singapore, this format is popularised by Japanese bento chains and fast-casual Japanese restaurants, offering an affordable and satisfying meal. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 185 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~9.8 g/100 g) from the seafood, moderate fat (~6.2 g/100 g) from the frying oil, and a significant carbohydrate load (~22.5 g/100 g) from the white rice. Salmon contributes omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin D (~4.2 mcg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (~1.4 mcg/100 g), making it nutritionally superior to purely starch-based bento options. However, the sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~1,395 mg per full serving) is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load per serving is high (~66), driven by the white rice component, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Allergens include fish, crustaceans, wheat (gluten), egg, and soy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a salmon prawn tempura bento?
How much sodium in a Japanese bento box?
Is salmon tempura bento high in fat?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA SR Legacy, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for a salmon-and-prawn tempura bento as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted blending of three USDA SR Legacy component entries (shrimp tempura, cooked Atlantic salmon, cooked white rice) in proportions reflecting a typical 450 g bento serving. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source (white rice) using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy component entries; no direct HPB or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry exists for this dish. A future direct laboratory analysis or HPB Foodo entry would upgrade confidence to high. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from the weighted USDA SR Legacy component blend; none are -1 as all were derivable from the component sources. 3. Halal status is marked false because standard tempura batter may contain mirin (fermented rice wine) and the frying oil provenance is unspecified; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the specific restaurant. 4. The input record listed cuisine_tags including Korean and Chinese, which are not applicable to this dish; these tags have been removed as the dish is Japanese in origin and preparation. 5. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; the University of Sydney GI Database does not contain a direct entry for this composite dish. 6. Serving size of 450 g is a typical estimate for a Japanese bento box of this type sold in Singapore; actual restaurant portions may vary. 7. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (65 × 101.25 g carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 ≈ 65.8, reported as 66.1 after rounding and condiment carbohydrate adjustment.
